Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a study routine. I'd mostly wait until I felt like something was due soon, then cram whatever I needed to learn. Being a kinesthetic learner, I found sitting at a computer for hours pretty draining, so I'd often grab my notes and pace around my room or go to the gym and think through concepts there. My study sessions were scattered and unpredictable, which I know isn't ideal.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place, really. Some weeks I wouldn't touch anything course-related, and then suddenly I'd have a burst of activity when deadlines loomed. With my extracurriculars taking up time and honestly just feeling kind of down a lot of the time, I never established any consistent schedule. It was always reactive rather than planned.
Q3: Usually it was panic about a deadline or getting a notification that something was due. Sometimes a peer would message me asking about an assignment, and that would remind me I should probably check what was going on. I rarely logged in just to browse or stay on top of things proactively. The LMS felt more like a necessary evil than a helpful tool.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. It never became routine for me. I'd go through phases where I'd log in more frequently, usually when the peer support group I was connected with would nudge me about things. But those moments were temporary, and I'd always drift back to my usual pattern of avoiding it until absolutely necessary.
Q5: The videos were probably the most useful because I could watch them while doing other things or moving around. I watched quite a bit of video content because it felt less like traditional studying. The peer interactions were helpful too when they happened—talking through concepts with others made them stick better than just reading. Discussion posts occasionally sparked something useful.
Q6: I definitely ignored a lot of the reading materials and supplementary resources. They felt overwhelming and passive, which doesn't work well for how I learn. I'd see long PDFs or articles and just close the tab, telling myself I'd get to them later. Later never really came most of the time. The forums I mostly lurked rather than participated in actively.
Q7: If I could see an immediate connection to an assignment or exam, it felt worth my time. Also, anything hands-on or interactive grabbed my attention more than passive content. When something felt like it would directly help me not fail, I'd engage with it. Abstract or optional stuff that seemed disconnected from grades just fell off my radar.
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff basically didn't exist to me. If it wasn't required, my brain just categorized it as ignorable. Required activities at least had the weight of consequences behind them. Optional things felt like suggestions I could safely ignore, especially when I was already feeling overwhelmed and sad about other things in life.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded a lot of materials and studied them away from my computer. I'd print things out or save PDFs to my phone and review them at the gym or while walking around campus. I also used external YouTube videos that explained concepts in more engaging ways. A lot of my actual learning happened completely offline.
Q10: There were many times I'd be working through problems on paper or practicing concepts physically—like literally acting out processes or using objects to understand relationships. None of that shows up anywhere. I'd also have long conversations with peers in person about course material that helped me understand things deeply, but the LMS would show nothing.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a video or PDF once, then work with it for days without logging back in. The LMS would show one quick access, but I might have spent hours with that material offline. It was just easier for me to study without being tethered to the platform, especially since I learn better when I'm moving around.
Q12: Mid-semester was rough. I was feeling really sad and my resilience was basically non-existent. When things got hard emotionally, the course felt impossible to care about. My attendance dropped, I stopped completing assignments on time, and logging into the LMS felt like climbing a mountain. The peer support helped a bit, but it was still a struggle.
Q13: Definitely. When I was tired or stressed from extracurriculars and life stuff, the LMS was the first thing I'd neglect. Confusion about what was actually required made me avoid logging in because I didn't want to face the overwhelm. Competing responsibilities always won over coursework because they felt more immediate or less anxiety-inducing.
Q14: Yeah, plenty of times I'd be scrolling through materials but my mind was completely elsewhere. I'd have videos playing but not actually absorbing anything because I was thinking about other problems in my life. The LMS would register my activity, but I wasn't really there mentally. It was just going through motions.
Q15: I postponed things constantly, even when I knew they mattered. The anxiety of starting something difficult made avoidance feel safer in the moment. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and tomorrow would become next week. With low resilience, facing challenging tasks felt insurmountable, so I'd distract myself with other things instead.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to act. Without them, I'd drift indefinitely. When a deadline approached, I'd suddenly become intensely focused and cram everything in. My LMS activity probably spiked dramatically right before due dates and then flatlined afterward. It wasn't healthy, but it's how I functioned.
Q17: When I got good feedback, it gave me a temporary boost and made me feel like maybe I could do this. But that feeling faded quickly. Negative or critical feedback sometimes made me avoid the course for a while because it confirmed my fears about not being good enough. The peer support helped me process feedback better than I would have alone.
Q18: The structure felt overwhelming at times with eleven courses to manage. When things were clearly laid out with explicit deadlines, I could function. But when expectations were vague or there were too many optional pathways, I'd freeze up and disengage. I needed more hand-holding than the course provided, honestly.
Q19: Having videos helped because they suited my learning style better than text. But the sheer volume of content made engagement harder. I wish there had been more interactive or practical components built into the LMS itself. The peer support intervention was helpful—without those connections, I probably would have disengaged even more.
Q20: I think that happens when people are anxious and trying to figure things out but can't bring themselves to commit to the actual work. You might be searching for answers, watching videos, reading discussions, but the act of submitting something feels too final and scary. It's like preparation paralysis—lots of motion, no output.
Q21: That's basically my story. My exam score was really high and I got a distinction, but my LMS activity was pretty minimal. I learned effectively offline and through peer conversations. The system only sees clicks and logins, not the actual understanding happening in my head. Some of us just don't need to be constantly online to learn.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of when people are active and what they click on. But they completely miss the quality of engagement, offline study, emotional state, and real understanding. They can't tell if someone watched a video attentively or had it playing in the background. They miss the human stuff that actually matters for learning.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when you find yourself thinking about course concepts outside of study time, or when you genuinely want to discuss ideas with others. For me, engagement happened in bursts and often away from any trackable platform. It's more mental than behavioral.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I struggled emotionally throughout the course, or how the peer support kept me from giving up entirely. It wouldn't capture my offline study habits, my conversations with classmates, or the fact that despite appearing disengaged online, I actually understood the material well enough to excel on exams. The sadness and anxiety that colored my whole experience is invisible to any tracking system.
